preFix.title: Re-capping chair and table feet
preFix.subtitle: Replace worn rubber ferrules and floor glides on furniture legs
preFix.excerpt: How to replace worn or missing rubber ferrules and plastic floor glides on chairs and tables: removing old caps with heat, preparing the leg end, selecting the correct replacement size, and fitting the new cap firmly so it does not work loose.
preFix.sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

content.text: that has split or fallen off leaves a raw timber leg end that scratches floors and accelerates leg wear. A

that has worn through leaves a plastic stub that scratches hard floors. Both are replaced in the same way; the only variable is which type suits the intended use: glides for hard floors, rubber ferrules for carpet or to fix a chair that slides too easily.

Replacement ferrules or floor glides in the correct size (measure the leg end with a tape measure or caliper: width and depth for square legs, diameter for round). A heat gun for removing old rubber ferrules stuck with age. A utility knife for trimming old adhesive pads. A sanding block for dressing the leg end before fitting. Contact adhesive for press-fit ferrules that are slightly loose.

Rubber ferrules that have hardened and stuck can be released by warming the rubber briefly with the heat gun set to low, then twisting and pulling off. Do not hold the heat in one place for more than 5 seconds; overheating can char the timber end. Self-adhesive felt or plastic glides can be peeled away; remove the adhesive residue with a cloth dampened with methylated spirit.

Sand the leg end flat and smooth with the sanding block. Remove any splinters or raised grain that would prevent the new cap from seating flush. Check the leg end dimensions again against the replacement part; a ferrule that is fractionally too small will split on fitting and one that is too large will work loose quickly.

For a press-fit rubber ferrule: push the ferrule onto the leg end and press down firmly; it should require moderate hand pressure and sit flush with no gap visible between the cap rim and the leg sides. If the ferrule is slightly loose, apply a thin bead of contact adhesive inside the ferrule and on the leg end, allow to tack for 2 minutes, then press together. For self-adhesive floor glides: peel the backing, centre the glide on the leg end, and press for 30 seconds. Stand the furniture on a hard floor and press down to seat each glide fully.
